title: The Contextual Revolution
concept: Dynamic representations solved polysemy, establishing contextual models as the default starting point for all modern NLP. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Transfer Learning.'

exercises.prompt: What makes words like 'bark', 'bank', and 'crane' tricky for basic computer models?

options.text: They are too long to process.
They have multiple meanings depending on the context.
They cannot be translated into other languages.

exercises.explain: Polysemy refers to words that look and sound the same but have different meanings depending on how they are used.

cards.title: Words with Many Faces
cards.body: Before the Contextual Revolution, AI models were easily confused by words with multiple meanings. A word like "apple" meant the exact same thing to an AI whether you were eating it or buying a smartphone! Contextual representations changed everything by teaching AI to read between the lines.

cards.title: What is the Contextual Revolution?
cards.body: The Contextual Revolution refers to the shift in NLP from using fixed word representations to dynamic representations. Instead of assigning a word a single permanent mathematical vector, modern models calculate a word's meaning dynamically based on all the other words surrounding it.

cards.title: The Magic Dictionary
cards.body: Think of early NLP models like a basic paper dictionary. The word "bank" has one printed page, and you have to guess which meaning fits. A contextual model is like a magical digital dictionary that reads the entire sentence first, then dynamically shifts its pages to show you only the perfect definition for that exact moment.

cards.title: Context in Action
cards.body: In the sentence "She decided to file the papers," the word "file" is calculated as a verb about organizing. In "He bought a new metal file," the exact same word "file" is represented as a noun describing a tool. The model automatically adjusts the mathematical representation for each sentence.

cards.title: No Giant Dictionaries Needed
cards.body: You might think AI models need a separate vocabulary list for every possible meaning of a word. In reality, they don't! The model uses the same vocabulary list but calculates fresh, custom mathematical representations on the fly as it reads each sentence.

cards.title: Static vs. Contextual
cards.body: Let's compare the old way of representing words to the new, contextual way that revolutionized NLP.

left.text: One fixed vector per word. 'Apple' (fruit) and 'Apple' (company) look identical to the computer.

right.text: Dynamic vectors calculated on the fly. The meaning shifts based on surrounding words.

exercises.prompt: Which linguistic problem is directly solved by dynamic contextual representations?
exercises.explain: Polysemy is the phenomenon where a single word has multiple meanings. Dynamic contextual representations solved this by adapting the word's meaning based on its context.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Before the Contextual Revolution, NLP models used static representations that gave the word 'run' the exact same meaning in 'run a marathon' and 'run a business'.
exercises.explain: Traditional static word vectors assigned one permanent mathematical representation to a word, no matter how it was used in a sentence.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the definition of modern NLP representations.
exercises.sentence: Dynamic representations solved polysemy by creating word meanings that are strictly [blank].
exercises.explain: Contextual representations look at the surrounding words to determine the specific meaning of a word in a sentence.

exercises.prompt: Two of these sentences show an AI understanding the context of the word 'crane'. Spot the AI that failed to use context and treated the word statically.

options.text: The tall crane lifted the heavy steel beam onto the roof of the skyscraper.
A beautiful white crane landed softly by the edge of the quiet lake.
The mechanical crane flew south for the winter with the other birds.

exercises.explain: Option C is AI-generated because it uses a fixed, static definition for 'crane' regardless of the context. Options A and B dynamically adapt the meaning to fit the sentence context.

exercises.prompt: How do contextual models actually solve polysemy to achieve human-like understanding?

options.text: They ignore grammar to process text faster.
They generate unique vector math for words based on their surrounding context.
They store billions of fixed definitions for every possible word.
They require human translators to approve word meanings in real time.

exercises.explain: Dynamic contextual representations allowed NLP models to understand nuances in human language like never before, making them the standard starting point for all modern language tasks. Next up: Transfer Learning.
